seo.title: Lopburi Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Lopburi Province: ancient Khmer ruins, the famous monkey temple Prang Sam Yot, King Narai's Palace, and sweeping sunflower fields. Area: 6,199 km², population: 752,000. Plan your visit.

overview: Lopburi is one of Thailand's oldest continuously inhabited cities, serving as a provincial capital in the central plains approximately 150 km north of Bangkok. The province is famous for three things above all else: its brazen troops of long-tailed macaques that have taken over the old town's Khmer ruins, the majestic 17th-century palace of King Narai the Great, and the vast sunflower fields that carpet the hillsides each November and December. With 11 districts and a history stretching back over a thousand years — through the Dvaravati, Khmer, Ayutthaya, and early Bangkok periods — Lopburi rewards visitors who want an authentic Thai historical experience well off the beaten path.
geography: Lopburi covers 6,199.8 km² of central Thailand's lower northern plains. The western portion is flat agricultural land irrigated by the Lopburi River (a tributary of the Chao Phraya), while the eastern edge rises into the foothills of the Phetchabun mountain range. The province borders eight neighboring provinces and is bisected by the main northern railway line. Khao Chin Lae hill, rising above sunflower fields in the northeast, is one of the province's most photogenic natural landmarks.
highlights: Phra Prang Sam Yot — 12th-century Khmer shrine tower overrun by hundreds of free-roaming long-tailed macaques, Lopburi's most iconic image
King Narai's Palace (Phra Narai Ratchaniwet) — 17th-century royal complex with three distinct zones, now housing the National Museum
Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat — grand Khmer-style prang opposite the train station, dating to the 13th century
Khao Chin Lae sunflower fields — thousands of acres of blooming sunflowers on a hillside, peak season November–January
Ban Wichayen — ruins of the residence built for Greek diplomat Constantine Phaulkon at King Narai's court
Annual Monkey Buffet Festival — held every November, local restaurants lay out a lavish fruit feast for the town's 3,000+ resident macaques
King Narai Festival — spectacular royal procession with elephants, traditional costumes, and folk performances at the palace every February

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool, dry season with temperatures of 22–32°C. Sunflower fields bloom November–January. The Monkey Buffet Festival (November) and King Narai Festival (February) both fall in this window. Ideal weather for exploring temples and outdoor ruins.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 36–40°C. Far fewer tourists. April's Songkran is celebrated locally. Morning temple visits are manageable but midday heat is intense.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon season brings heavy afternoon rain and lush green landscapes. Humidity is high. The ruins and palaces are less crowded and hotel prices drop. Some sunflower field roads become muddy in October.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the perfect combination of cool weather, blooming sunflowers, and major festivals. The Monkey Buffet Festival in late November is a uniquely Lopburi experience. Weekday visits to Prang Sam Yot avoid weekend tour-group crowds.

topAttractions.name: Phra Prang Sam Yot (Monkey Temple)
topAttractions.description: Lopburi's most photographed landmark — a trio of laterite and sandstone Khmer prangs (tower-shrines) built in the 12th–13th century, originally as a Hindu shrine and later converted to Buddhism. Today hundreds of long-tailed macaques treat the ruins as their permanent home, clambering over every surface. The monkey troops can be bold; keep bags zipped and sunglasses on. A must-see despite its modest size.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB foreigners (10 THB Thais). Combined ticket for Prang Sam Yot + Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat + Ban Wichayen: 150 THB

topAttractions.name: Phra Narai Ratchaniwet (King Narai's Palace)
topAttractions.description: A vast 17th-century royal palace complex built by King Narai the Great (r. 1656–1688), who made Lopburi his second capital and welcomed French ambassadors from Louis XIV here. The walled compound is divided into outer, middle, and inner zones containing audience halls, royal pavilions, and warehouses. The central Phiman Mongkut Pavilion now houses the Lopburi National Museum with Khmer sculptures and royal regalia. The King Narai Festival takes place here each February.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 150 THB (includes National Museum). Open Wed–Sun 8:30 AM–4:30 PM

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat
topAttractions.description: A large Khmer-era prang visible directly opposite Lopburi train station, making it most visitors' first sight of the city. The main tower dates to the 13th century with later Ayutthaya-period additions including a large wihan (assembly hall). The brick prangs, Buddha images, and surrounding chedis make for excellent photography, especially at golden hour. Combination ticket available with nearby Prang Sam Yot.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB foreigners. Combined ticket with Prang Sam Yot + Ban Wichayen: 150 THB

topAttractions.name: Ban Wichayen (Constantine Phaulkon's Residence)
topAttractions.description: Evocative ruins of the mansion built for Constantine Phaulkon, the Greek adventurer who rose to become King Narai's chief minister in the 1680s and brokered relations with France. The compound includes a European-style residence, a Jesuit chapel, and servants' quarters — a rare blend of Western Baroque and Thai architecture in 17th-century Siam. Phaulkon's dramatic fall from power during the 1688 Siamese Revolution ended in his execution.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB foreigners. Combined ticket with Prang Sam Yot + Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat: 150 THB. Open 8:30 AM–4:30 PM

topAttractions.name: Khao Chin Lae Sunflower Fields
topAttractions.description: Thailand's most famous sunflower landscape — thousands of acres of sunflowers planted across hillside fields in Chai Badan district, approximately 60 km from Lopburi city. The flowers peak between mid-November and late January, turning the hillsides brilliant yellow. Come at sunrise for the best light and fewest crowds. Some fields charge a small entry fee (20–50 THB) during peak bloom; access roads are paved but narrow.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free to 50 THB depending on field (Nov–Jan peak season)

topAttractions.name: Lopburi National Museum (in King Narai's Palace)
topAttractions.description: Housed inside the Phiman Mongkut Pavilion within the palace complex, this museum holds one of central Thailand's finest collections of Lopburi-style Khmer sculpture, Dvaravati Buddhist art, and artifacts from King Narai's reign including gifts from Louis XIV of France. Two additional buildings display weapons, royal regalia, and folk objects. Essential context for understanding the ruins scattered across the old town.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 150 THB (combined with palace grounds). Open Wed–Sun 8:30 AM–4:30 PM

localFood: Kai Khem (salted eggs) — Lopburi's most famous export; duck eggs preserved in brine for weeks, producing a firm, rich yolk sold at markets throughout the province
Khanom Chin — fresh rice-flour noodles served cold with a choice of coconut curry, fish sauce, or spicy green nam prik sauces; a staple morning street food in the old town
Tom Yam Nam Sai (clear tom yam) — the local style uses river fish and a lighter, peppery broth without coconut milk, showcasing central-plains produce
Look Chin Pla (fish balls) — hand-pounded freshwater fish balls grilled on skewers or served in broth, common at Lopburi's morning market near the train station
Khanom Mo Kaeng — a baked Thai custard cake made with eggs, coconut cream, and palm sugar; sold at local dessert shops around King Narai's Palace
Nam Phrik Kapi (shrimp-paste relish) — pounded in a mortar and served with fresh vegetables and fried fish; the local version uses Lopburi River shrimp paste with a more robust flavor
Kai Yang (grilled marinated chicken) — roadside vendors near the bus terminal serve whole spatchcocked chickens grilled over charcoal, eaten with sticky rice and som tam
culture: Lopburi carries over a thousand years of layered history — Dvaravati Buddhist art, Khmer Hindu-Buddhist architecture, and the cosmopolitan 17th-century Narai court that hosted French diplomats and a Greek chief minister. The city's most vivid cultural tradition today is its relationship with its monkey population: the long-tailed macaques are considered sacred guardians of the Prang Sam Yot shrine, and the annual Monkey Buffet Festival (last Sunday of November) sees local businesses sponsor a giant fruit and vegetable feast for the animals. The King Narai Festival each February fills the palace grounds with elephant processions, period costume parades, classical dance performances, and traditional crafts, recreating court life of the 1680s.
gettingThere: Lopburi is 153 km north of Bangkok and is one of the easiest provincial day trips or overnights from the capital. Frequent trains run from Krung Thep Aphiwat (Bang Sue Grand Station) and Hua Lamphong throughout the day; journey time is approximately 2–2.5 hours and fares range from 28 THB (3rd class) to around 180 THB (2nd class air-con). The train station sits at the edge of the old town, within walking distance of all main ruins. Buses depart Bangkok's Mo Chit (Chatuchak) terminal several times daily, taking 2.5–3 hours and costing around 100–130 THB. Driving via Highway 1 (Phahon Yothin) takes about 2 hours depending on traffic. There is no commercial airport in Lopburi.
gettingAround: The old town containing all major ruins is compact enough to explore on foot — Prang Sam Yot, King Narai's Palace, Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat, and Ban Wichayen are all within 1 km of each other. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks with benches) run along Wichayen Road and Phra Narai Maharat Road connecting the old and new town areas (10–20 THB per trip). Motorcycle taxis queue outside the train station. Renting a bicycle or motorcycle (150–250 THB/day) from guesthouses near the old town is the best option for reaching the sunflower fields at Khao Chin Lae, which requires your own transport or a booked taxi/tuk-tuk.
travelTips: Protect food and belongings around Prang Sam Yot — the macaques will snatch sunglasses, bags, and anything edible without warning
Buy the 150 THB combination ticket at the first site you visit — it covers Prang Sam Yot, Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat, and Ban Wichayen and saves 30 THB over individual entry
The King Narai's Palace museum is closed Monday and Tuesday — plan accordingly
Train is the best way to arrive: the station is next to Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat, putting you in the heart of the old town instantly
Sunflower fields peak from mid-November through January — go on a weekday morning for solitude and the best golden-hour light
Lopburi works well as a day trip from Bangkok (train there, explore, train back) or as an overnight en route to Phitsanulok or Ayutthaya
The Monkey Buffet Festival (last Sunday of November) is spectacular but draws large crowds — book accommodation weeks ahead
